1.49: . a. Jesus chose the twelve Apostles.

2.1: In those days a decree was issued by Caesar Augustus for a census of all the earth.
2.2: This first census took place while Quirinius was in command of Syria.
2.3: And everyone was going to be counted, each in their own town.
2.4: Joseph went up from Galilee, from the town of Nazareth, to Judea, to the city of David, which is called Bethlehem, because he belonged to the house and lineage of David,
2.5: to be registered with Marie, his wife, who was pregnant.
2.6: But while they were in that place, the time came for her to give birth.
2.7: And she gave birth to her firstborn son, wrapped him in swaddling clothes and laid him in a manger, because there was no room for them in the inn.
2.8: In the surrounding area, there were shepherds who spent the night in the fields, watching over their flocks.
2.9: Suddenly an angel of the Lord appeared to them, and the radiance of the glory of the Lord surrounded them, and they were filled with great fear.
2.10: But the angel said to them, «Do not be afraid, for I bring you good news that will cause great joy for all the people.
2.11: For unto you is born this day in the city of David a Savior, who is Christ the Lord.
2.12: And this will be a sign to you: you will find a newborn baby wrapped in swaddling cloths and lying in a manger.»
2.13: At that very moment, a multitude of the heavenly host joined the angel, praising God and saying:
2.14: «"Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace to men of good will."»
2.15: When the angels had left them, ascending into heaven, the shepherds said to one another, «Let us go over to Bethlehem and see this thing that has happened, which the Lord has told us about.»
2.16: They went there in haste and found Mary, Joseph, and the newborn baby lying in the manger.
2.17: After seeing him, they recounted the revelation that had been made to them concerning this Child.
2.18: And all who heard them were amazed at what the shepherds told them.
2.19: Now Mary treasured all these things, pondering them in her heart.
2.20: And the shepherds returned, glorifying and praising God for all they had seen and heard, just as it had been told them.
2.21: When the eight days were completed, for the circumcision of the Child, he was called Jesus, the name which the angel had given him before he was conceived in the womb.
2.22: Then, when the days of their purification were completed, according to the law of Moses, Mary and Joseph brought the Child to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord
2.23: according to what is written in the law of the Lord: «Every firstborn male shall be consecrated to the Lord,»
2.24: and to offer as a sacrifice, as prescribed by the law of the Lord, a pair of turtledoves, or two small pigeons.
2.25: Now there was a man in Jerusalem named Simeon, a righteous man who feared God, who was waiting for the consolation of Israel, and the Holy Spirit was upon him.
2.26: The Holy Spirit had revealed to him that he would not die before seeing the Lord's Christ.
2.27: So he came into the temple, moved by the Spirit. And as the parents brought in the infant Jesus to observe the legal customs concerning him,
2.28: He too received him in his arms and blessed God, saying:
2.29: Now, O Master, you let your servant depart in peace, according to your word,
2.30: since my eyes have seen your salvation,
2.31: that you have prepared in the sight of all peoples:
2.32: light to dispel the darkness of the nations and glory of Israel, your people.
2.33: The Child's father and mother were in awe of the things that were said about him.
2.34: And Simeon blessed them and said to Mary, his mother: «This child is born for the falling and rising of many in Israel, and to be a sign that will be spoken against,
2.35: "A sword will pierce your own soul, and thus the thoughts hidden in the hearts of many will be revealed."»
2.36: There was also a prophetess, Hannah, daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Asher; she was very old, having lived seven years with her husband since her virginity.
2.37: Having remained a widow and reached the age of eighty-four, she did not leave the temple, serving God night and day in fasting and prayer.
2.38: She too, arriving at that hour, began to praise the Lord and to speak of the Child to all those in Jerusalem who were awaiting redemption.
2.39: When they had done everything according to the law of the Lord, they returned to Galilee, to Nazareth, their own town.
2.40: However, the child grew and became strong, being filled with wisdom, and the grace of God was upon him.
2.41: But his parents went to Jerusalem every year for the Passover festival.
2.42: When he reached his twelfth year, they went up there, according to the custom of this festival,
2.43: And when they returned home, the days of the festival having passed, the child Jesus remained in the city, without his parents having noticed.
2.44: Thinking he was with their traveling companions, they walked all day, then they searched for him among their relatives and acquaintances.
2.45: Not having found him, they returned to Jerusalem to look for him.
2.46: After three days they found him in the temple, sitting among the teachers, listening to them and questioning them.
2.47: And all who heard him were delighted by his intelligence and his answers.
2.48: When they saw him, they were astonished, and his mother said to him, "My child, why have you treated us like this? Your father and I have been searching for you anxiously."«
2.49: And he answered them, «Why were you looking for me? Did you not know that I must be in my Father’s house?»
2.50: But they did not understand what he was telling them.
2.51: So he went down with them and came to Nazareth and was obedient to them. And his mother treasured all these things in her heart.
2.52: And Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favor with God and men.

3.1: In the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, when Pontius Pilate was governor of Judea, Herod tetrarch of Galilee, Philip his brother tetrarch of Ituraea and the region of Trachonitis, and Lysanias tetrarch of Abilene,
3.2: In the days of the high priests Annas and Caiaphas, the word of the Lord came to John, son of Zechariah, in the desert.
3.3: And he went throughout the region of the Jordan, preaching repentance baptism for the forgiveness of sins,
3.4: as it is written in the book of the oracles of the prophet Isaiah: «A voice heard in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the Lord, make his paths straight.
3.5: Every valley shall be filled in, every mountain and hill shall be lowered, the crooked roads shall become straight and the rough ones leveled.
3.6: And all flesh shall see God's salvation.»
3.7: He said to those who were flocking to be baptized by him: «Brood of vipers, who warned you to flee from the wrath to come?
3.8: Therefore bear worthy fruits of repentance and do not try to say to yourselves: Abraham is our father, for I tell you that from these very stones God can raise up children to Abraham.
3.9: The axe is already at the root of the trees. Therefore, every tree that does not bear good fruit will be cut down and thrown into the fire.»
3.10: And the people asked him, "What then must be done?"«
3.11: He replied, "Whoever has two tunics should give one to the one who has none, and whoever has food should do likewise."«
3.12: Tax collectors also came to be baptized, and they said to him, «Teacher, what should we do?»
3.13: He told them, "Do not demand anything beyond what you are commanded."«
3.14: Soldiers also questioned him, saying, "And what should we do?" He replied, "Refrain from all violence and fraud, and be content with your pay."«
3.15: As the people waited in expectation and all wondered in their hearts, with regard to John, whether he might be the Christ,
3.16: John said to them all, «I baptize you with water, but one who is more powerful than I is coming, whose sandal strap I am not worthy to untie. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire.
3.17: His hand holds the winnowing fork, and he will clear his threshing floor, and he will gather the wheat into his barn, and he will burn up the chaff with unquenchable fire.»
3.18: Through these exhortations and many others like them, he was therefore announcing the good news to the people.
3.19: But Herod the tetrarch, being rebuked by him concerning Herodias, his brother's wife, and all the evil he had done,
3.20: He added this crime to all the others and imprisoned Jean.
3.21: Now, at the time when all the people had just received baptism, Jesus was also baptized, and while he was praying, the heavens were opened,
3.22: And the Holy Spirit descended upon him in bodily form, like a dove, and a voice came from heaven, saying, «You are my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.»
3.23: Jesus was about thirty years old when he began his ministry; he was, as was believed, the son of Joseph, the son of Heli.,
3.24: son of Matthat, son of Levi, son of Melchi, son of Janneh, son of Joseph,
3.25: son of Mattathias, son of Amos, son of Nahum, son of Hesli, son of Naggé,
3.26: son of Maath, son of Mattathias, son of Shemei, son of Josech, son of Judah,
3.27: son of Joanan, son of Resa, son of Zerubbabel, son of Shealtiel, son of Neri,
3.28: son of Melchi, son of Addi, son of Cosam, son of Elmadam, son of Her,
3.29: son of Jesus, son of Eliezer, son of Jorim, son of Matthat, son of Levi,
3.30: son of Simeon, son of Judah, son of Joseph, son of Jonan, son of Eliakim,
3.31: son of Melea, son of Menna, son of Mattatha, son of Nathan, son of David,
3.32: son of Jesse, son of Obed, son of Boaz, son of Salmon, son of Nahshon,
3.33: son of Amminadab, son of Aram, son of Hezron, son of Perez, son of Judah,
3.34: son of Jacob, son of Isaac, son of Abraham, son of Terah, son of Nahor,
3.35: son of Sarug, son of Reu, son of Peleg, son of Eber, son of Salé,
3.36: son of Cainan, son of Arphaxad, son of Shem, son of Noah, son of Lamech,
3.37: son of Methuselah, son of Enoch, son of Jared, son of Malaleel, son of Cainan,
3.38: son of Enos, son of Seth, son of Adam, son of God.

4.1: Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan and was led by the Spirit into the desert,
4.2: For forty days, he was subjected to the temptations of the devil. He ate nothing during those days, and when they were over, he was hungry.
4.3: Then the devil said to him, "If you are the Son of God, command this stone to become bread."«
4.4: Jesus answered him, «It is written: »Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word of God.’”
4.5: And the devil, having taken him to a high mountain, showed him in an instant all the kingdoms of the earth,
4.6: and said to him, «I will give you all this power and all the glory of these kingdoms, for it has been delivered to me and I give it to whomever I want.
4.7: »If you bow down before me, she will be entirely yours.”
4.8: Jesus answered him, «It is written: »You shall worship the Lord your God and him only shall you serve.’”
4.9: The demon led him back to Jerusalem and, having placed him on the pinnacle of the temple, said to him, «If you are the Son of God, throw yourself down from here.”
4.10: For it is written: His angels have been commanded concerning you to guard you
4.11: and they will take you into their hands, lest your foot strike against the stone.»
4.12: Jesus answered him, «It is written: »You shall not put the Lord your God to the test.’”
4.13: After tempting him in every way, the devil withdrew from him for a time.
4.14: Then Jesus returned to Galilee in the power of the Spirit, and his fame spread throughout the surrounding countryside.
4.15: He taught in their synagogues and they all praised him.
4.16: Having come to Nazareth, where he had been brought up, he entered the synagogue on the Sabbath day, according to his custom, and stood up to read.
4.17: He was given the scroll of the prophet Isaiah, and unrolling it, he found the place where it was written:
4.18: «The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to bring good news to the poor, and he has sent me to heal the brokenhearted.”,
4.19: to proclaim freedom for the captives and recovery of sight for the blind, to set the oppressed free, to proclaim the year of the Lord’s favor.»
4.20: Having rolled up the book, he returned it to the attendant and sat down, and all in the synagogue had their eyes fixed on him.
4.21: Then he began to tell them, «Today your ears have heard the fulfillment of this passage of Scripture.»
4.22: And all bore witness to him, and marveling at the gracious words that came from his mouth, they said, «Is this not the son of Joseph?»
4.23: And he said to them, «No doubt you will cite to me this adage: Physician, heal thyself, and you will tell me: The great things we have heard you did in Capernaum, do them here in your homeland.»
4.24: And he added: «Truly I tell you, no prophet is well received in his homeland.
4.25: Truly I tell you, there were many widows in Israel in the days of Elijah, when the heavens were shut up for three and a half years, and there was a great famine throughout the land,
4.26: And yet Elijah was not sent to any of them, but to a widow in Zarephath, in the land of Sidon.
4.27: Likewise, there were many lepers in Israel in the days of the prophet Elisha, and yet none of them were healed, but Naaman the Syrian was.»
4.28: When they heard this, they were all filled with anger in the synagogue.
4.29: And getting up, they drove him out of the city and led him to a craggy edge of the mountain on which their city was built, to throw him down.
4.30: But he, passing through the midst of them, went away.
4.31: He went down to Capernaum, a town in Galilee, and there he taught them on the Sabbath days.
4.32: And his doctrine astonished them, because he spoke with authority.
4.33: There was a man in the synagogue possessed by an unclean demon, who cried out loudly,
4.34: saying, «Leave me alone, what do we have to do with you, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are: the Holy One of God.»
4.35: But Jesus said sternly to him, «Be quiet and come out of him.» And the demon threw him to the ground in the midst of the assembly and came out of him without harming him.
4.36: And they were all amazed and said to one another, «What is this saying? With authority and power he commands the unclean spirits, and they come out.»
4.37: And his fame spread throughout the country.
4.38: Then Jesus got up, left the synagogue, and went into Simon’s house. Now Simon’s mother-in-law was suffering from a high fever, and they prayed to him for her.
4.39: Bending over the sick woman, he commanded the fever and the fever left her and, having risen at once, she began to serve them.
4.40: When the sun had set, all those who had sick people in their homes, whatever their illness, brought them to him, and Jesus laid his hand on each one of them and healed them.
4.41: Demons also came out of many, shouting and saying, "You are the Son of God," and he rebuked them to silence, because they knew that he was the Christ.
4.42: As soon as day broke, he went out and left for a deserted place. A crowd of people went looking for him, and when they came to him, they wanted to keep him from leaving them.
4.43: But he said to them, «I must proclaim the kingdom of God to the other towns also, because that is what I was sent for.»
4.44: And Jesus preached in the synagogues of Galilee.

5.1: Now, one day, pressed by the crowd who wanted to hear the word of God, he was standing on the shore of the Sea of Galilee,
5.2: He saw two boats moored near the shore; the fishermen had gone ashore to wash their nets.
5.3: So he got into one of the boats, which was Simon’s, and asked him to put out a little from the shore, and then, sitting down, he taught the people from the boat.
5.4: When he had finished speaking, he said to Simon, «Put out into the deep sea and let down your nets for a catch.»
5.5: Simon answered him, «Master, we have worked all night and caught nothing, but because you say so, I will let down the nets.»
5.6: Having cast it, they caught such a large quantity of fish that their net began to break.
5.7: And they signaled to their companions, who were in the other boat, to come to their aid. They came and filled both boats so full that they began to sink.
5.8: When Simon Peter saw this, he fell at Jesus' feet, saying, "Go away from me, Lord, for I am a sinner."«
5.9: For terror had seized him and all those who accompanied him because of the catch of fish they had made,
5.10: The same was true of James and John, sons of Zebedee, Simon's partners. And Jesus said to Simon, "Do not be afraid, for from now on you will be catching men."«
5.11: Immediately, bringing their boats back to shore, they left everything and followed him.
5.12: While he was in a town, a man covered with leprosy saw Jesus and fell with his face to the ground and begged him, «Lord, if you are willing, you can make me well.»
5.13: Jesus, stretching out his hand, touched him and said to him, "I will it, be healed," and at once his leprosy disappeared.
5.14: And he forbade him to speak of it to anyone, but said: «Go,» he said, “show yourself to the priest and offer for your healing what Moses prescribed, to testify to it before the people.”
5.15: His fame spread more and more, and large crowds came to hear him and to be cured of their illnesses.
5.16: For him, it was a matter of retreating to the deserts and praying.
5.17: One day while he was teaching, Pharisees and teachers of the Law from all the villages of Galilee, as well as from Judea and Jerusalem, were sitting around him, and the power of the Lord was present to heal them.
5.18: And then some people, carrying a paralyzed man on a bed, were trying to bring him in and place him in front of him.
5.19: And not finding a way because of the crowd, they went up onto the roof and, through the tiles, lowered the sick man with his stretcher into the midst of everyone, in front of Jesus.
5.20: Seeing their faith, he said, «Man, your sins are forgiven.»
5.21: Then the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason and say, «Who is this who utters blasphemies? Who can forgive sins but God alone?»
5.22: Jesus, knowing their thoughts, spoke to them and said, «What are you thinking in your hearts?
5.23: Which is easier, to say: Your sins are forgiven, or to say: Get up and walk?
5.24: But so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins—he said to the paralytic, »I tell you, get up, take your mat and go home.”
5.25: At that moment, he got up in front of them, took the bed on which he had been lying, and went home, glorifying God.
5.26: And they were all amazed; they glorified God and, filled with awe, said, «We have seen wonderful things today.»
5.27: After this, Jesus went out and saw a tax collector named Levi sitting at the tax collector’s booth, and said to him, «Follow me.»
5.28: And he left everything, got up, and followed him.
5.29: Levi gave him a great feast in his house, and a large crowd of tax collectors and others were at table with them.
5.30: The Pharisees and their scribes murmured and said to his disciples, «Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?»
5.31: Jesus answered them, «It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick.
5.32: I have not come to call the righteous to repentance, but sinners.»
5.33: Then they said to him, «Why is it that the disciples of John and of the Pharisees fast and pray often, but yours eat and drink?»
5.34: He answered them, «Can you make the friends of the Bridegroom fast while the Bridegroom is with them?”
5.35: The days will come when the Bridegroom will be taken away from them, and they will fast on those days.»
5.36: He offered them this further comparison: "No one puts a piece taken from a new garment onto an old garment: otherwise the new garment is torn and the piece from the new garment does not fit the old garment well.
5.37: No one puts new wine into old wineskins; otherwise the new wine will burst the skins and spill out, and the skins will be ruined.
5.38: But the new wine must be put into new wineskins and both will keep.
5.39: And no one, after drinking old wine, immediately wants new wine, because it is said: 'Old wine is better.'»

6.1: One Sabbath day, as Jesus was passing through fields of wheat, his disciples picked ears of grain and, crushing them in their hands, ate them.
6.2: Some Pharisees said to them, «Why are you doing what is not lawful on the Sabbath day?»
6.3: Jesus answered them, «Have you not read what David did when he and his companions were hungry?
6.4: How did he enter the house of God and take the consecrated bread, eat it, and give it to those who were with him, even though it was only permitted for the priests to eat it?»
6.5: And he added, «The Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath.»
6.6: Another Sabbath, Jesus entered the synagogue and was teaching. And a man was there whose right hand was withered.
6.7: Now the Scribes and Pharisees were watching him closely to see if he was performing healings on the Sabbath day, so that they might have a pretext to accuse him.
6.8: But he, perceiving their thoughts, said to the man with the withered hand, «Get up and stand in the middle,» and he got up and stood.
6.9: Then Jesus said to them, «I ask you, is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do evil, to save life or to take it away?»
6.10: Then, looking around at them all, he said to the man, "Stretch out your hand." He stretched it out, and his hand was healed.
6.11: But they, filled with fury, were plotting what to do to Jesus.
6.12: In those days, he withdrew to the mountain to pray and spent the whole night praying to God.
6.13: When day came, he called his disciples and chose twelve of them, whom he named apostles:
6.14: Simon, whom he named Peter, and Andrew his brother, James and John, Philip and Bartholomew,
6.15: Matthew and Thomas, James, son of Alphaeus, and Simon, called the Zealot,
6.16: Jude, brother of James and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.
6.17: Having gone down with them, he stopped on a plateau, where there was a crowd of his disciples and a great multitude of people from all Judea, from Jerusalem and from all the coastal region of Tyre and Sidon.
6.18: They had come to hear him and to be healed of their illnesses. Those who were tormented by unclean spirits were healed.
6.19: And all these people sought to touch him, because a virtue emanated from him that healed them all.
6.20: Then, looking up at his disciples, he said to them, «Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of heaven.
6.21: Blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied. Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh.
6.22: Blessed are you when people hate you, exclude you from their company, heap reproach upon you, and reject your name as evil, because of the Son of Man.
6.23: Rejoice in that day and leap for joy, for behold, your reward is great in heaven: for thus did their fathers treat the prophets.
6.24: But woe to you rich people, for you have received your consolation.
6.25: Woe to you who are well fed now, for you will go hungry. Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep.
6.26: Woe to you when everyone speaks well of you, for that is what their ancestors did to the false prophets.
6.27: But I tell you who are listening: Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you.
6.28: Bless those who curse you and pray for those who mistreat you.
6.29: If someone strikes you on one cheek, turn to him the other also, and if someone takes your coat from you, do not prevent him from taking your tunic as well.
6.30: Give to anyone who asks you, and if your property is taken from you, do not demand it back.
6.31: Do to others as you would have them do to you.
6.32: If you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? Even sinners love those who love them.
6.33: And if you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? Even sinners do the same.
6.34: And if you lend to those from whom you expect to receive, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, expecting to be repaid in full.
6.35: As for you, love your enemies, do good and lend without expecting anything in return and your reward will be great and you will be children of the Most High, who is kind to the ungrateful and the wicked.
6.36: Therefore be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.
6.37: Do not judge and you will not be judged, do not condemn and you will not be condemned, forgive and it will be forgiven you.
6.38: Give, and it will be given to you. A good measure, pressed down, shaken together and running over, will be poured into your lap. For with the measure you use, it will be measured to you.»
6.39: He made this comparison to them: «Can a blind man lead a blind man? Will they not both fall into the pit?”
6.40: The disciple is not above the master, but every disciple, when his instruction is complete, will be like his master.
6.41: Why do you look at the speck of sawdust in your brother’s eye and do not notice the plank in your own eye?
6.42: Or how can you say to your brother, «Brother, let me take the speck out of your eye,” when you yourself do not see the plank in your own eye? You hypocrite, first take the plank out of your own eye, and then see clearly to take the speck out of your brother’s eye.
6.43: Indeed, there is no good tree that bears bad fruit, nor a bad tree that bears good fruit.,
6.44: Each tree is recognized by its fruit. You don't pick figs from thorns, you don't cut grapes from brambles.
6.45: A good man brings good things out of the good treasure of his heart, and an evil man brings evil things out of the evil treasure of his heart, for the mouth speaks what the heart is full of.
6.46: Why do you call me Lord, Lord, and do not do what I say?
6.47: Everyone who comes to me and listens to my words and puts them into practice, I will show you what he is like.
6.48: It is like a man who, building a house, dug deep beforehand and laid its foundation on rock. When a flood came, the torrent crashed against that house and could not shake it, because it had its foundation on rock.
6.49: But the one who listens and does not put into practice is like a man who built his house on the ground without a foundation; the torrent came and struck it, and immediately it collapsed, and great was the ruin of that house.»

7.1: After he had finished saying all his words to the people, Jesus entered Capernaum.
7.2: Now, a centurion had a sick servant who was going to die, and he loved him very much.
7.3: Having heard about Jesus, he sent Jewish leaders to him, asking him to come and heal his servant.
7.4: When they came to Jesus, they begged him earnestly, saying, «He deserves that you do this for him,
7.5: "Because he loves our nation and he even built our synagogue."»
7.6: So Jesus went with them. He was not far from the house when the centurion sent some of his friends to say to him, «Lord, do not trouble yourself, for I am not worthy to have you come under my roof,
7.7: Therefore I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you, but say the word and my servant shall be healed.
7.8: For I myself am a man under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell this one, 'Go,' and he goes; and that one, 'Come,' and he comes; and that one, 'Do this,' and he does it.»
7.9: When Jesus heard this, he was amazed at the man and, turning to the crowd following him, said, «Truly I tell you, I have not found anyone in Israel with such great faith.»
7.10: Upon their return to the centurion's house, the messengers found the servant who had been ill healed.
7.11: Some time later, Jesus went to a town called Nain, and many disciples and a large crowd went with him.
7.12: As he approached the city gate, he found that a dead man was being carried out, the only son of his mother, and she was a widow, and many people from the city were accompanying her.
7.13: When the Lord saw her, he was moved with compassion for her and said to her, «Do not weep.»
7.14: And approaching, he touched the coffin, the pallbearers having stopped, then he said: "Young man, I command you, get up."«
7.15: Immediately the dead man sat up and began to speak, and Jesus gave him back to his mother.
7.16: They were all filled with awe and they glorified God, saying, «A great prophet has appeared among us, and God has visited his people.»
7.17: And this word spoken about him spread throughout all Judea and all the surrounding country.
7.18: John's disciples having reported all these things to him,
7.19: He called two of them and sent them to Jesus to say, «Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?»
7.20: So they came to him and said, «John the Baptist sent us to you to ask, »Are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else?’”
7.21: At that very moment, Jesus healed a great number of people afflicted by illness, infirmity, or evil spirits, and gave sight to several blind people.
7.22: Then he answered the messengers: «Go and report to John what you have seen and heard: the blind receive their sight, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor are preached to them.
7.23: Blessed is the one who is not offended by me.»
7.24: When John's messengers had left, Jesus began to speak to the people about John: "What did you go out into the desert to see? A reed shaken by the wind?
7.25: What did you go out into the desert to see? A man dressed in soft clothes? But those who wear fine clothes and live in luxury are in royal palaces.
7.26: So what did you go to see? A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and more than a prophet.
7.27: It is of him that it is written: I am sending my messenger before your face, to go before you and prepare the way for you.
7.28: For I tell you, among those born of women there is no prophet greater than John the Baptist, but the least in the kingdom of God is greater than he.
7.29: All the people who heard him, including the tax collectors, justified God by being baptized with the baptism of John.,
7.30: while the Pharisees and the teachers of the law rejected God's plan for them by not being baptized by him.»
7.31: «To whom then,” the Lord continued, “shall I compare the men of this generation? To whom are they like?”
7.32: They are like children sitting in the marketplace, calling out to one another and saying: We played the flute for you and you did not dance, we sang dirges for you and you did not cry.
7.33: For John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say, 'He has a demon.'.
7.34: The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and you say, 'Here is a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners.'.
7.35: But Wisdom has been vindicated by all her children.»
7.36: A Pharisee having asked Jesus to eat with him, he went into his house and sat down at the table.
7.37: And behold, a woman who was leading a dissolute life in the city, having learned that he was reclining at table in the Pharisee's house, brought an alabaster jar full of perfume,
7.38: And standing behind him at his feet, weeping, she began to sprinkle them with her tears and to wipe them with the hair of her head, and she kissed them and anointed them with perfume.
7.39: At this sight, the Pharisee who had invited him said to himself, "If this man were a prophet, he would know who and what kind of woman this is who is touching him, and that she is a sinner."«
7.40: Then Jesus spoke up and said to him, «Simon, I have something to tell you.» «Teacher, speak,» he said.
7.41: A creditor had two debtors, one owed five hundred denarii and the other fifty.
7.42: Since they had no way to pay their debt, he forgave them both. Which of them will love him more?»
7.43: Simon replied, "I suppose the one to whom he forgave the larger sum." Jesus said to him, "You have judged correctly."«
7.44: And turning to the woman, he said to Simon, «Do you see this woman? I entered your house, and you did not pour water on my feet, but she has wet them with her tears and wiped them with her hair.
7.45: You didn't give me a kiss, but she, ever since I came in, has kept kissing my feet.
7.46: You did not anoint my head with oil, but she anointed my feet with perfume.
7.47: Therefore, I tell you, her many sins have been forgiven her, because she loved much; but he who is forgiven little loves little.»
7.48: Then he said to the woman, "Your sins are forgiven."«
7.49: And those who were at the table with him began to say to themselves, "Who is this who even forgives sins?"«
7.50: But Jesus said to the woman, «Your faith has saved you; go in peace.»

8.1: Then Jesus traveled through the towns and villages, preaching and announcing the good news of the kingdom of God. The Twelve were with him,
8.2: as well as some women who had been cured of evil spirits and diseases: Mary, called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had come out,
8.3: Joanna, wife of Chusa, Herod's steward, Susanna, and several others, who supported him from their resources.
8.4: A large crowd having gathered, and people having come to him from various towns, Jesus said in a parable:
8.5: «"The sower went out to sow, and as he was sowing, some fell along the path and was trampled underfoot, and the birds of the air ate it up.
8.6: Another part fell onto the stone and, as soon as it was lifted, it dried, because it had no moisture.
8.7: Another part fell among the thorns, and the thorns, growing with it, suffocated it.
8.8: Another part fell on good soil, and when it sprouted, it produced a hundredfold.» Speaking these words, he said aloud, «He who has ears to hear, let him hear.»
8.9: His disciples asked him what this parable meant:
8.10: «To you,” he said, “the knowledge of the mystery of the kingdom of God has been given, but to others it is spoken in parables, so that, seeing they do not see, and hearing they do not understand.”.
8.11: This is what this parable means: The seed is the word of God.
8.12: Those along the way are the ones who hear the word, but then the devil comes and takes it away from their hearts, lest they should believe and be saved.
8.13: Those in whom the seed was sown on the rock are those who, hearing the word, receive it with joy, but they have no root: they believe for a while and in the hour of testing fall away.
8.14: What fell on the thorns represents those who, having heard the word, gradually allow themselves to be suffocated by the worries, riches and pleasures of life and they do not reach maturity.
8.15: Finally, what fell on good soil represents those who, having heard the word with a good and excellent heart, keep it and bear fruit by perseverance.
8.16: No one, after lighting a lamp, covers it with a vessel or puts it under a bed, but puts it on a lampstand, so that those who enter may see the light.
8.17: For there is nothing hidden that will not be discovered, nothing secret that will not eventually be known and come to light.
8.18: Therefore, be careful how you listen, for to the one who has, more will be given, and from the one who does not have, even what he thinks he has will be taken away.»
8.19: Jesus' mother and brothers came to see him, but they could not get in because of the crowd.
8.20: They came and told him, "Your mother and brothers are outside and they want to see you."«
8.21: He replied, "My mother and brothers are those who hear the word of God and put it into practice."«
8.22: One day, Jesus got into a boat with his disciples and said to them, "Let's cross to the other side of the lake." And they set out to sea.
8.23: While they were sailing, he fell asleep and a whirlwind of wind having struck the lake, their boat filled with water and they were in peril.
8.24: So they approached him and woke him, saying, «Master, Master, we are perishing!» He got up and rebuked the wind and the agitated waves, and they calmed down and there was peace.
8.25: Then he said to them, «Where is your faith?» They were filled with fear and amazement, saying to one another, «Who then is this, that he commands the wind and the sea, and they obey him?»
8.26: They then landed in the country of the Gerasenes, which is opposite Galilee.
8.27: When Jesus stepped ashore, a man from the town who had long been possessed by demons came to meet him; he wore no clothes and had no other dwelling than the tombs.
8.28: As soon as he saw Jesus, he cried out and came to his feet, saying in a loud voice, «What do you have to do with me, Jesus, Son of God, Most High? Please, do not torment me.»
8.29: Indeed, Jesus commanded the unclean spirit to come out of the man. Many times the spirit had seized him, and although he was kept bound with chains and shackles on his feet, he would break his bonds and the demon would drive him out into deserted places.
8.30: Jesus asked him, «What is your name?» He said to him, «My name is Legion,» because many demons had entered him.
8.31: And these demons begged Jesus not to command them to go into the abyss.
8.32: Now, there was a large herd of pigs grazing on the mountain, and they begged him to allow them to enter, and he allowed them.
8.33: So, coming out of the man they went into the pigs and the herd, taking its run, rushed down the steep slopes into the lake and drowned there.
8.34: At this sight, the guards fled and carried the news throughout the town and countryside.
8.35: The people went out to see what had happened: they came to Jesus and found the man from whom the demons had gone out, sitting at his feet, clothed and sane in spirit, and they were filled with fear.
8.36: Those who had witnessed it also told them how the demoniac had been delivered.
8.37: Then all the inhabitants of the country of the Gerasenes begged him to leave them, because they were filled with great fear. So Jesus got into the boat to return.
8.38: Now the man from whom the demons had gone out begged him to admit him to his side, but Jesus sent him away, saying:
8.39: «Go back home and tell everyone how much God has done for you.» So he went away and told all over town what Jesus had done for him.
8.40: When Jesus returned, he was welcomed by the people, for they were all waiting for him.
8.41: And behold, a man named Jairus, who was a leader of the synagogue, came and fell at Jesus' feet, begging him to come into his house,
8.42: because he had an only daughter, about twelve years old, who was dying.
8.43: As Jesus was walking there and was pressed by the crowd, a woman who had suffered from a flow of blood for twelve years and who had spent all her money on doctors, without any being able to cure her,
8.44: She approached him from behind and touched the fringe of his coat. Instantly, his bleeding stopped.
8.45: And Jesus said, «Who touched me?» They all denied it, but Peter and those with him said, «Master, the crowd is surrounding you and pressing against you, and you are asking, »Who touched me?’”
8.46: But Jesus said, "Someone touched me, for I felt power go out from me."«
8.47: Seeing herself discovered, the woman came trembling and threw herself at his feet and told everyone why she had touched him and how she had been instantly healed.
8.48: And Jesus said to her, «Daughter, your faith has saved you; go in peace.»
8.49: While he was still speaking, someone from the synagogue leader's household came to him and said, "Your daughter is dead; do not trouble the Teacher."«
8.50: When Jesus heard this, he replied to the father, «Do not be afraid; only believe, and she will be saved.»
8.51: When he arrived home, he let no one in with him except Peter, James and John, with the child's father and mother.
8.52: But everyone was weeping and mourning for her, and Jesus said, «Do not weep, she is not dead, but asleep.»
8.53: And they mocked him, knowing full well that she was dead.
8.54: But he, taking her by the hand, said aloud, "Child, get up."«
8.55: And her spirit returned to her and she got up at once, and Jesus ordered that she be given something to eat.
8.56: His parents were overjoyed, but he advised them not to tell anyone what had happened.

9.1: Having gathered the Twelve together, Jesus gave them power and authority over all demons and the power to heal diseases.
9.2: And he sent them out to preach the kingdom of God and to heal the sick,
9.3: and he said to them, «Take nothing for the journey, neither staff, nor bag, nor bread, nor money, and do not have two tunics.
9.4: Whatever house you enter, stay there until you leave that place.
9.5: If they refuse to receive you, leave that city and shake the dust from your feet as a testimony against them.»
9.6: The disciples went from village to village, preaching the Gospel and performing healings everywhere.
9.7: However, Herod the tetrarch heard about all that Jesus was doing, and he did not know what to think, for some were saying, "John has risen from the dead,",
9.8: Others say, "Elijah has appeared," others say, "One of the ancient prophets has risen from the dead."«
9.9: Herod said, "As for John, I had him beheaded. Who then is this man about whom I hear such things?" And he sought to see him.
9.10: The apostles returned and told Jesus everything they had done. He took them with him and withdrew by himself to a solitary place near a town called Bethsaida.
9.11: When the people heard about it, they followed him. Jesus welcomed them and spoke to them about the kingdom of God, and he healed those who were sick.
9.12: As the day was drawing to a close, the Twelve came to him and said, «Send the people away so that they may spread out into the surrounding villages and hamlets and find shelter and food, for we are here in a desolate place.»
9.13: He replied, "You give them something to eat." They said to him, "We only have five loaves of bread and two fish, unless perhaps we ourselves go and buy enough to feed all these people."«
9.14: For there were about five thousand men. Jesus said to his disciples, «Have them sit down in groups of fifty.»
9.15: They obeyed him and made them sit down.
9.16: Then Jesus took the five loaves and the two fish, and looking up to heaven, he gave thanks, broke them, and gave them to his disciples to serve the people.
9.17: They all ate and were satisfied, and of the leftover pieces, they took away twelve baskets.
9.18: One day, while he was praying in a solitary place, with his disciples with him, he asked them this question: "Who do the crowds say I am?"«
9.19: They replied: "John the Baptist, others Elijah, others that one of the ancient prophets has risen again.
9.20: »And you,« he asked them, »who do you say I am?” Peter replied, “The Christ of God.”
9.21: But he sternly ordered them not to tell anyone.
9.22: «It is necessary,» he added, “that the Son of Man suffer many things and be rejected by the elders, the chief priests and the scribes, and that he be killed and on the third day be raised to life.”
9.23: Then, addressing everyone, he said: «If anyone wants to come after me, let him deny himself, take up his cross daily, and follow me.
9.24: For whoever wants to save their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life for my sake will save it.
9.25: What does it profit a man to gain the whole world, and yet forfeit himself?
9.26: And if anyone is ashamed of me and my words, the Son of Man will be ashamed of him when he comes in his glory and in the glory of the Father and of the holy angels.
9.27: Truly I tell you, some who are standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God.»
9.28: About eight days after he said these words, Jesus took with him Peter, James and John and went up on the mountain to pray.
9.29: While he was praying, the appearance of his face changed and his clothes became dazzlingly white.
9.30: And then two men were talking with him: they were Moses and Elijah.,
9.31: Appearing in glory, they discussed his death, which was to take place in Jerusalem.
9.32: Peter and his companions were overcome with sleep, but having stayed awake, they saw the glory of Jesus and the two men who were with him.
9.33: As they were leaving him, Peter said to Jesus, «Master, it is good for us to be here. Let us put up three shelters, one for you, one for Moses and one for Elijah.» He did not know what he was saying.
9.34: As he spoke these words, a cloud came and overshadowed them, and the disciples were terrified as they entered the cloud.
9.35: And a voice came from the cloud, saying, «This is my beloved Son; listen to him.»
9.36: While the voice was speaking, Jesus was alone. The disciples remained silent and told no one at that time what they had seen.
9.37: The next day, when they came down from the mountain, a large crowd came to meet Jesus.
9.38: And a man cried out from the midst of the crowd, "Master, I beg you, take a look at my son, for he is my only child.
9.39: A spirit seizes him and immediately he cries out, the spirit violently agitates him, making him foam at the mouth, and scarcely leaves him after having bruised him all over.
9.40: I asked your disciples to drive him out, but they could not.
9.41: »O unbelieving and perverse generation,” Jesus replied, “how long will I be with you and put up with you? Bring your son here.”
9.42: And as the child approached, the demon threw him to the ground and shook him violently.
9.43: But Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, healed the child, and gave him back to his father.
9.44: And they were all amazed at the greatness of God. While everyone was marveling at what Jesus was doing, he said to his disciples, «Listen carefully to this: The Son of Man is going to be delivered into the hands of men.»
9.45: But they did not understand this saying; it was veiled to them, so that they did not have the understanding and they were afraid to question him about it.
9.46: Then a thought crept into their minds: which of them was the greatest.
9.47: Jesus, seeing the thoughts of their hearts, took a little child and laid him by his side.,
9.48: And he said to them, «Whoever welcomes this little child in my name welcomes me, and whoever welcomes me welcomes the one who sent me. For the one among you who is least is the one who is great.»
9.49: John, speaking up, said: «Teacher, we saw someone driving out demons in your name, and we tried to stop him because he was not one of us.»
9.50: »Do not stop him,” Jesus replied, “for whoever is not against you is for you.”
9.51: When the days of his being taken from the world were near, he resolved to go to Jerusalem.
9.52: He sent messengers ahead of him, who set out and entered a Samaritan village to prepare for his arrival.,
9.53: But the inhabitants refused to receive him, because he was heading towards Jerusalem.
9.54: When his disciples James and John saw this, they said, «Lord, do you want us to call down fire from heaven to consume them?»
9.55: Jesus turned and rebuked them, saying, «You do not know what kind of spirit you are.»
9.56: The Son of Man did not come to destroy lives, but to save them.» And they went to another village.
9.57: While they were on their way, a man said to him, "I will follow you wherever you go."«
9.58: Jesus answered him, «Foxes have dens and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay his head.»
9.59: He said to another, "Follow me." The man replied, "Lord, first let me go and bury my father."«
9.60: But Jesus said to him, «Let the dead bury their own dead, but go and proclaim the kingdom of God.»
9.61: Another said to him, "I will follow you, Lord, but first let me go and say goodbye to those of my household."«
9.62: Jesus answered him, «No one who puts a hand to the plow and looks back is fit for service in the kingdom of God.»

10.1: After that, the Lord appointed seventy-two others and sent them on ahead of him, two by two, into all the cities and places where he himself was to go.
10.2: He told them, «The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few. Therefore pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out workers into his harvest field.”.
10.3: Go: behold, I am sending you out like lambs in the midst of wolves.
10.4: Carry no purse, no bag, no sandals, and greet no one on the road.
10.5: Whatever house you enter, first say, "Peace to this house."«
10.6: And if there is a child of peace there, your peace will rest upon him; if not, it will return to you.
10.7: Stay in the same house, eating and drinking whatever they have, for the worker deserves his wages. Do not move from one house to another.
10.8: In whatever town you enter, if you are welcomed, eat what is offered to you.,
10.9: Heal the sick who are there and tell them: The kingdom of God is near to you.
10.10: But in whatever town you enter, if they do not welcome you, go out into the public squares and say:
10.11: Even the dust of your city, which has clung to us, we wipe away against you; yet know this, that the kingdom of God is near.
10.12: I tell you, on that day there will be less severity for Sodom than for this city.
10.13: Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the miracles that were performed in you had been performed in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.
10.14: That is why, at the judgment, there will be less severity for Tyre and Sidon than for you.
10.15: And you, Capernaum, who exalt yourself to heaven, you will be brought down to hell.
10.16: »Whoever listens to you listens to me, and whoever despises you despises me; and whoever despises me despises the one who sent me.”
10.17: The seventy-two returned with joy, saying, "Lord, even the demons submit to us in your name."«
10.18: He replied to them, "I saw Satan falling from the sky like lightning.
10.19: Behold, I have given you the power to trample on serpents and scorpions and all the power of the enemy, and it shall not be able to harm you in any way.
10.20: »Do not rejoice that the spirits submit to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven.”
10.21: At that same time, he leaped for joy through the Holy Spirit and said: «I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and prudent and revealed them to little children. Yes, I praise you, Father, for this is what you were pleased to do.”.
10.22: »All things have been handed over to me by my Father, and no one knows what the Son is except the Father, and what the Father is except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him.”
10.23: And turning to his disciples, he said to them privately, «Blessed are the eyes that see what you see.
10.24: For I tell you, many prophets and kings longed to see what you see but did not see it, and to hear what you hear but did not hear it.»
10.25: And behold, a lawyer stood up and asked him to test him, «Teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life?»
10.26: Jesus said to him, «What is written in the Law? How do you read it?»
10.27: He replied, «You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your strength, and with all your mind, and your neighbor as yourself.»
10.28: Jesus said to him, «You have answered correctly; do this and you will live.»
10.29: But this man, wanting to justify himself, said to Jesus, "And who is my neighbor?"«
10.30: Jesus continued: «A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and he fell into the hands of robbers, who stripped him and beat him severely, and departed, leaving him half dead.
10.31: Now, it happened that a priest was coming down the same path, he saw this man and passed by.
10.32: Likewise, a Levite, having come to that place, approached, saw him, and passed on by.
10.33: But a Samaritan, who was traveling, came near him and, seeing him, was moved with compassion.
10.34: He approached, bandaged his wounds, after pouring oil and wine on them, then he put him on his own mount, took him to an inn and took care of him.
10.35: The next day, taking out two denarii, he gave them to the innkeeper and said to him: Take care of this man and whatever more you spend, I will repay you on my return.
10.36: Which of these three do you think was the next man to fall into the hands of the robbers?
10.37: The doctor replied, "The one who showed mercy to him." And Jesus said to him, "You also go and do likewise."«
10.38: While they were on their way, Jesus entered a village and a woman named Martha welcomed him into her home.
10.39: She had a sister named Mary, who sat at the Lord's feet and listened to his teaching.,
10.40: While Martha was busy with the various tasks of serving, she stopped and said, «Lord, do you not care that my sister has left me to do the work alone? Tell her to help me.»
10.41: The Lord answered her, «Martha, Martha, you are worried and upset about many things.
10.42: Only one is necessary. Mary has chosen the better part, which will not be taken away from her.»

11.1: One day, while Jesus was praying in a certain place, when he had finished, one of his disciples said to him, "Lord, teach us to pray, as John taught his disciples."«
11.2: He told them, «When you pray, say: Father, hallowed be your name, your kingdom come.
11.3: Give us this day the bread we need to sustain ourselves,
11.4: "Forgive us our trespasses, for we ourselves forgive everyone who trespasses against us, and lead us not into temptation."»
11.5: He also said to them, «If one of you has a friend and goes to him at midnight, saying, ‘Friend, lend me three loaves of bread,’,
11.6: because a friend of mine who is travelling has arrived at my house and I have nothing to offer him.,
11.7: and that from inside the house, the other replies: Don't bother me, the door is already closed, my children and I are in bed, I can't get up to give you some:
11.8: I tell you, even if he would not get up to give him anything because he is his friend, he will get up because of his shamelessness and give him as much bread as he needs.
11.9: And I tell you: Ask and it will be given to you, seek and you will find, knock and the door will be opened to you.
11.10: For everyone who asks receives, and everyone who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks it will be opened.
11.11: Which of you fathers, if your son asks for bread, will give him a stone? Or if he asks for a fish, will give him a snake instead?
11.12: Or, if he asks her for an egg, will he give her a scorpion?
11.13: If you then, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him!»
11.14: Jesus was casting out a demon, and this demon was mute. When the demon was expelled, the mute man spoke, and the people were amazed.
11.15: But some of them said, "It is by Beelzebul, prince of demons, that he drives out demons."«
11.16: Others, to test him, asked him for a sign in the sky.
11.17: Knowing their thoughts, Jesus said to them, «Every kingdom divided against itself will be destroyed, and houses will fall one upon another.
11.18: If Satan is divided against himself, how can his kingdom stand? For you say that it is by Beelzebul that I drive out demons.
11.19: And if I cast out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your sons cast them out? Therefore they will be your judges.
11.20: But if it is by the finger of God that I cast out demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.
11.21: When a strong and well-armed man guards the entrance to his house, what he owns is safe.
11.22: But if someone stronger comes along and defeats him, he takes away all the weapons in which he trusted and divides his spoils.
11.23: Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters.
11.24: When an unclean spirit leaves a man, it goes through arid places, seeking rest. Not finding any, it says, “I will return to my house from which I came.”.
11.25: And when he arrives, he finds it cleaned and decorated.
11.26: Then he goes away, taking with him seven other spirits more wicked than himself, and they go in and dwell there. And the last state of that man becomes worse than the first.»
11.27: As he was speaking, a woman from the crowd raised her voice and said to him, "Blessed is the womb that bore you and the breasts that you suckled."«
11.28: Jesus replied, «Blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and keep it.»
11.29: When the people gathered in crowds, he began to say, «This generation is a wicked generation; it asks for a sign, but none will be given it except the sign of the prophet Jonah.
11.30: For just as Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites, so the Son of Man will be a sign to this generation.
11.31: The Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with the men of this generation and condemn them, because she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon: and there is something greater than Solomon here.
11.32: The men of Nineveh will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, because they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and something greater than Jonah is here.
11.33: No one lights a lamp and puts it in a hidden place or under a basket, but on a lampstand, so that those who enter may see the light.
11.34: Your eye is the lamp of your body. If your eye is healthy, your whole body will be full of light; if it is unhealthy, your body will be full of darkness.
11.35: Therefore, beware lest the light that is in you become darkness.
11.36: »If your whole body is in the light, without any mixing of darkness, it will be fully illuminated, as when the light of a lamp shines upon you.”
11.37: While he was speaking, a Pharisee invited him to dinner at his house, and Jesus went in and sat down at the table.
11.38: But the Pharisee was astonished to see that he had not performed ablution before dinner.
11.39: The Lord said to him, «Now you Pharisees clean the outside of the cup and of the dish, but inside you all is full of greed and iniquity.
11.40: Fools! Did not he who made the outside make the inside also?
11.41: However, give alms according to your means and everything will be pure for you.
11.42: But woe to you Pharisees, who tithe mint, rue, and every garden plant, and have no concern for justice and the love of God. These you ought to have done, without neglecting the others.
11.43: Woe to you, Pharisees, who love the best seats in the synagogues and greetings in the marketplaces.
11.44: Woe to you, for you are like unseen tombs, and people walk over them without knowing it.»
11.45: Then a lawyer spoke up and said to him, "Teacher, by speaking in this way you insult us also."«
11.46: Jesus replied, «Woe to you also, teachers of the law! Because you load people down with burdens hard to bear, and you yourselves do not lift a finger to help them.”.
11.47: Woe to you, who build tombs for the prophets, and it was your fathers who killed them.
11.48: So you are witnesses and you applaud the works of your fathers, for they killed them and you build tombs for them.
11.49: That is why the Wisdom of God said: I will send them prophets and apostles, they will kill some of them and persecute others:
11.50: so that this generation may be held accountable for the blood of all the prophets that has been shed since the creation of the world,
11.51: from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who was killed between the altar and the sanctuary. Yes, I tell you, this generation will be held accountable for it.
11.52: »Woe to you, experts in the law, because you have taken away the key to knowledge; you yourselves have not entered, and you have hindered those who were entering.”
11.53: As Jesus was saying these things to them, the Pharisees and the scribes began to press him sharply and to overwhelm him with questions,
11.54: setting traps for him and seeking to catch him in a word to accuse him.

12.1: Meanwhile, when thousands of people had gathered, to the point of trampling one another, Jesus began to say to his disciples: «Beware above all of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.
12.2: There is nothing hidden that should not be revealed, nothing secret that should not be known.
12.3: Therefore, everything you have said in the dark will be heard in the daylight, and what you have whispered in the ear inside the house will be proclaimed from the housetops.
12.4: But I tell you, my friends: Do not be afraid of those who kill the body and after that can do no more.
12.5: I will teach you whom you should fear: fear him who, after killing, has the power to throw into hell; yes, I tell you, fear him.
12.6: Are not five sparrows worth two aces? And not one of them is forgotten by God.
12.7: But even the hairs on your head are all numbered. So don't be afraid: you are worth more than many sparrows.
12.8: I tell you again, whoever confesses me before men, the Son of Man will also confess him before the angels of God,
12.9: But whoever denies me before men will be denied before the angels of God.
12.10: And whoever speaks against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven.
12.11: When you are brought before the synagogues, the magistrates, and the authorities, do not worry about how you will defend yourselves or what you will say.,
12.12: For the Holy Spirit will teach you at that very hour what you ought to say.»
12.13: Then someone from the crowd said to Jesus, "Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me."«
12.14: Jesus answered him, «Man, who appointed me to be a judge or to divide your property?»
12.15: And he said to the people, «Be on your guard against all kinds of greed, for even in abundance, a person’s life does not consist in the wealth they possess.»
12.16: Then he told them this parable: «There was a rich man whose land produced an abundant harvest.
12.17: And he was discussing these thoughts to himself: What shall I do? For I have no place to store my harvest.
12.18: “This is what I will do,” he said. “I will tear down my barns and build bigger ones, and there I will store all my harvests and possessions.”.
12.19: And I will say to my soul: My soul, you have great riches laid up for many years; take rest, eat, drink, and be merry.
12.20: But God said to him, “Fool! This very night your soul will be demanded from you, and what you have stored up for yourself; whose will it be?”
12.21: So it is with the man who stores up treasures for himself and is not rich before God.»
12.22: Then Jesus said to his disciples, «Therefore I tell you, do not worry about your life, what you will eat; or about your body, what you will wear.
12.23: Life is more than food and the body is more than clothing.
12.24: Consider the ravens: they neither sow nor reap, they have neither storehouse nor barn, and yet God feeds them. How much more valuable are you than these birds?
12.25: Which of you, through constant worrying, could add an arm to the length of your life?
12.26: If even the smallest things are beyond your power, why worry about others?
12.27: Consider the lilies, how they grow: they neither labor nor spin; and I tell you, Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.
12.28: If that is how God clothes the grass, which is today in the field and tomorrow is thrown into the fire, how much more will he clothe you, you of little faith.
12.29: You too, don't worry about what you'll eat or drink and don't be anxious.
12.30: For it is the people of this world who are concerned about these things, but your Father knows that you need them.
12.31: But seek first the kingdom of God, and all these things will be given to you as well.
12.32: Do not be afraid, little flock, for it is more pleasing to your Father to give you the kingdom.
12.33: Sell what you have and give alms. Make for yourselves purses that time does not wear out, an inexhaustible treasure in heaven, where thieves do not enter and moths do not destroy.
12.34: For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.
12.35: Keep your belt tight around your waist and your lights on.
12.36: Be like men who are waiting for their master to return from the wedding banquet, so that as soon as he arrives and knocks at the door, they may open it for him at once.
12.37: Blessed are those servants whom the master finds awake when he returns. Truly I tell you, he will dress himself to serve, will have them recline at the table, and will come and wait on them.
12.38: Whether he arrives at the second watch, or at the third, if he finds them thus, blessed are those servants.
12.39: But you should know that if the father of the family knew what time the thief was coming, he would be vigilant and would not let his house be broken into.
12.40: You too must be ready, because the Son of Man will come at an hour you do not expect.»
12.41: Then Peter said to him, «Are you telling this parable to us, or to everyone?»
12.42: The Lord replied, «Who is the faithful and wise manager whom the master will put in charge of his servants, to distribute the measure of wheat at the proper time?”
12.43: Blessed is that servant whom the master finds so doing when he comes.
12.44: I tell you truly, he will establish it over all his possessions.
12.45: But if that servant says to himself, 'My master is taking a long time to come,' and begins to beat the other servants, both men and women, and to eat and drink and get drunk,
12.46: The master of that servant will come on a day when he does not expect him and at an hour he does not know, and will have him torn with blows and assign him a place with the unbelievers.
12.47: That servant who knew his master's will and did not prepare anything or act according to his will will receive many blows.
12.48: But he who has not known it and has done things deserving of punishment will receive few blows. Much will be demanded of him to whom much has been given, and the more one has entrusted to someone, the more will be asked of him.
12.49: I have come to cast fire upon the earth, and how I desire that it already be kindled.
12.50: I still have to be baptized, and what anguish I feel until it is accomplished.
12.51: Do you think I came to establish peace on earth? No, I tell you, but rather division.
12.52: For now, if there are five people in one house, they will be divided, three against two and two against three,
12.53: The father will be divided against his son and the son against his father, the mother against her daughter and the daughter against her mother, the mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law and the daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.»
12.54: He also told the people: "When you see a cloud rising in the west, you immediately say: Rain is coming, and so it happens.
12.55: And when you see the south wind blowing, you say: It will be hot, and it is.
12.56: Hypocrites, you know how to recognize the aspects of heaven and earth, how then can you not recognize the time in which we live?
12.57: And how is it that you do not discern for yourselves what is right?
12.58: Indeed, when you go with your adversary before the magistrate, try on the way to free yourself from his pursuit, lest he drag you before the judge and the judge hand you over to the officer of the court and the latter throw you into prison.
12.59: I'm telling you, you won't get out of here until you've paid every last penny.»

13.1: At that time some people came and told Jesus what had happened to the Galileans, whose blood Pilate had mixed with that of their sacrifices.
13.2: He answered them, «Do you think that these Galileans were greater sinners than all the other Galileans, because they suffered in this way?
13.3: No, I tell you, but unless you repent, you will all perish as they did.
13.4: Or those eighteen on whom the tower of Siloam fell and whom it killed, do you think their debt was greater than that of all the other inhabitants of Jerusalem?
13.5: No, I tell you, but unless you repent, you will all perish as well.»
13.6: He also told this parable: «A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard. He went to look for fruit on it, but did not find any,
13.7: He said to the winegrower: For three years I have been coming to look for fruit on this fig tree and I find none, cut it down then: why does it make the land unproductive?
13.8: The vinedresser replied: Lord, let it alone for one more year, until I have dug up and put manure all around it.
13.9: Perhaps it will bear fruit later; if not, you can cut it down.»
13.10: Jesus was teaching in a synagogue on the Sabbath.
13.11: Now, there was a woman there who had been possessed for eighteen years by a spirit that made her infirm: she was bent over and could not straighten up at all.
13.12: When Jesus saw her, he called her and said, «Woman, you are set free from your infirmity.»
13.13: And he laid his hands on her, and immediately she sat up and glorified God.
13.14: But the synagogue leader, indignant that Jesus had performed this healing on the Sabbath, spoke up and said to the people, «There are six days for work, so come and be healed on those days and not on the Sabbath day.
13.15: "Hypocrite," the Lord replied, "doesn't each of you on the Sabbath untie your ox or donkey from its stall and lead it out to water?"
13.16: And this daughter of Abraham, whom Satan had kept bound for eighteen years, should not be released from this bondage on the Sabbath day.»
13.17: While he was speaking, all his opponents were covered in confusion, and all the people were delighted by all the wonderful things he was accomplishing.
13.18: He also said, «What is the kingdom of God like, and to what shall I compare it?
13.19: It is like a mustard seed, which a man took and planted in his garden; it grew and became a tree, and the birds of the air made their homes in its branches.»
13.20: He also said, «To what shall I compare the kingdom of God?”
13.21: It is like the leaven that a woman takes and mixes with three measures of flour, so that the whole dough rises.»
13.22: So he went through the towns and villages, teaching and making his way towards Jerusalem.
13.23: Someone asked him, "Lord, will only a few be saved?" He told them:
13.24: «Strive to enter through the narrow door, for many, I tell you, will try to enter and will not be able.”.
13.25: Once the head of the household has gotten up and closed the door, if you are outside and start knocking, saying: Lord, open the door for us. He will answer you: I don't know where you are from.
13.26: Then you will begin to say: We ate and drank in your presence, and you taught in our public squares.
13.27: And he will answer you: I tell you, I do not know where you come from; depart from me, all you workers of iniquity.
13.28: Then there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth, when you see Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, while you are thrown out.
13.29: People will come from the East and the West, from the North and the South, and they will take their places at the banquet in the kingdom of God.
13.30: And those who are last will be first, and those who are first will be last.»
13.31: That same day some Pharisees came to him and said, «Get away from here, because Herod wants to kill you.»
13.32: He replied to them: "Go and tell that fox: I drive out demons and heal the sick today and tomorrow, and on the third day I will have finished.
13.33: But I must continue on my way today and tomorrow and the day after, for it is not fitting that a prophet should die outside of Jerusalem.
13.34: Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to her! How often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing!.
13.35: »Your house is left to you. I tell you, you will not see me again until the day comes when you say, ‘Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.’”

14.1: One Sabbath, Jesus went into the house of one of the leading Pharisees to eat, and they were watching him closely.
14.2: And there before him stood a man suffering from dropsy.
14.3: Jesus, speaking up, said to the teachers of the law and the Pharisees, «Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?»
14.4: And they remained silent. He, taking the man by the hand, healed him and sent him away.
14.5: Then, addressing them, he said, «Which of you, if your donkey or ox falls into a well, does not immediately pull it out on the Sabbath day?»
14.6: And to that they knew only how to reply.
14.7: Then, noticing the eagerness of the guests to choose the best seats, Jesus told them this parable:
14.8: «When you are invited by someone to a wedding feast, do not take the place of honor, lest there be a man more distinguished than you.”
14.9: and let not the one who invited you both come and say to you: Give him the seat and then let you not begin in confusion to occupy the last place.
14.10: But when you are invited, go and sit in the lowest place, so that when the one who invited you comes, he will say to you, 'Friend, move up higher.' Then it will be an honor for you in the presence of the other guests.
14.11: For whoever exalts himself will be humbled, and whoever humbles himself will be exalted.
14.12: He also said to the one who had invited him, «When you give a dinner or a supper, do not invite your friends, your brothers, your relatives, or rich neighbors, lest they invite you in turn and repay you for what they have received from you.
14.13: But when you give a feast, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, and the blind,
14.14: And you will be glad that they cannot repay you, for you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.»
14.15: One of those who were at the table with him, having heard these words, said to Jesus, «Blessed is the one who will share in the banquet in the kingdom of God.»
14.16: Jesus said to him, «A man gave a great banquet and invited many people.
14.17: At mealtime, he sent his servant to tell the guests: Come, for everything is now ready.
14.18: And all of them, unanimously, began to apologize. The first one said to him: I have bought some land and I need to go and see it, please excuse me.
14.19: The second one said: I bought five pairs of oxen and I'm going to try them out, please excuse me.
14.20: Another said: I have just gotten married and that is why I cannot go.
14.21: The servant returned and reported these things to his master. Then the head of the household, angered, said to his servant, “Go quickly to the streets and lanes of the city and bring here the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.”.
14.22: The servant said, "Master, it has been done as you commanded, and there is still room.".
14.23: The master said to the servant, “Go out into the roads and hedges and compel those you find to come in, so that my house may be filled.”.
14.24: For I tell you, none of these men who were invited will taste my feast.»
14.25: As a large crowd was walking with him, he turned around and said to them:
14.26: «If anyone comes to me and does not hate his father and mother, his wife and children, his brothers and sisters, and even his own life, he cannot be my disciple.
14.27: And whoever does not carry his cross and follow me cannot be my disciple.
14.28: For which of you, intending to build a tower, does not first sit down and estimate the cost, and whether he has enough to complete it?
14.29: For fear that, having laid the foundations of the building, he might not be able to bring it to completion and that all who see it might begin to mock him,
14.30: saying: This man began to build and he was unable to finish.
14.31: Or what king, if he is going to war against another king, does not first sit down and consider whether he can, with ten thousand men, face an enemy who comes to attack him with twenty thousand?
14.32: If he cannot do so, while the latter is still far away, he sends him an embassy to negotiate peace.
14.33: Therefore, whoever among you does not renounce all that he has cannot be my disciple.
14.34: Salt is good, but if salt loses its flavor, how can we give it back its taste?
14.35: Useless for both soil and manure, it is thrown out. Let anyone who has ears hear.»

15.1: All the tax collectors and sinners were approaching Jesus to hear him.
15.2: And the Pharisees and the scribes murmured, saying, «This man welcomes sinners and eats with them.»
15.3: Then he told them this parable:
15.4: «Which of you, having a hundred sheep, if he loses one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine others in the wilderness, and go after the one that is lost, until he finds it?”
15.5: And when he found her, he joyfully put her on his shoulders,
15.6: And when he returned home, he gathered his friends and neighbors together and said to them, “Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep which was lost.”.
15.7: Therefore, I tell you, there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance.
15.8: Or what woman, having ten drachmas, if she loses one, does not light a lamp, sweep her house, and search carefully until she finds it?
15.9: And when she found it, she gathered her friends and neighbors and said to them: Rejoice with me, because I have found the drachma that I had lost.
15.10: Therefore, I tell you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner who repents.»
15.11: He also said: "A man had two sons.
15.12: The younger son said to his father, “Father, give me my share of the estate.” So the father divided his estate between them.
15.13: A few days later, the youngest son, having gathered all that he had, left for a distant country and there squandered his wealth living in debauchery.
15.14: When he had spent everything, a great famine came upon that country, and he began to feel need.
15.15: So he went away and entered the service of a resident of that country, who sent him to his country house to tend the pigs.
15.16: He would have liked to fill his stomach with the pods that the pigs were eating, but nobody gave him any.
15.17: Then, coming to his senses, he said: How many of my father’s hired servants have more than enough bread, while I am dying here of hunger.
15.18: I will get up and go to my father and say to him, “Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you,”,
15.19: I no longer deserve to be called your son: treat me like one of your workers.
15.20: And he got up and went to his father. As he was still a long way off, his father saw him and, deeply moved, ran up, threw himself on his neck and covered him with kisses.
15.21: His son said to him, "Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you; I am no longer worthy to be called your son.".
15.22: But the father said to his servants, “Bring the best robe and dress him, put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet.”.
15.23: Bring also the fattened calf and kill it, let us have a celebratory feast:
15.24: For this son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found. And they began to celebrate.
15.25: Now the older son was in the fields, and as he came back and approached the house, he heard music and dancing.
15.26: Calling one of the servants over, he asked him what it was.
15.27: The servant told him, "Your brother has arrived, and your father has killed the fattened calf, because he has found it safe and sound.".
15.28: But he became angry and refused to go in. So the father went out and began to pray to him.
15.29: He replied to his father: "For so many years I have served you, and have never disobeyed your orders, yet you have never given me a young goat to celebrate with my friends.".
15.30: And when that other son, who has devoured your property with prostitutes, arrives, you kill the fattened calf for him.
15.31: The father said to him: You, my son, are always with me and everything I have is yours.
15.32: But we had to celebrate and rejoice, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.»
